pathophysiology_summary: Ultraviolet (predominantly UVB) exposure of unprotected skin causes direct keratinocyte DNA damage and release of inflammatory mediators (prostaglandins, cytokines, nitric oxide), producing cutaneous vasodilatation (erythema, heat), sensitisation of nociceptors (pain) and interstitial oedema (the tight, drawing sensation). In a first-degree (superficial epidermal) burn the epidermis remains intact — there is brisk complete blanching, no blister formation and no dermal involvement, distinguishing it from a partial-thickness (second-degree) burn. Onset is typically 4-6 hours post-exposure, peaks around 24 hours and resolves over several days with pruritus and fine desquamation as the epidermis heals. The distribution strictly follows sun-exposed skin with a demarcation line at clothing edges. There are no systemic features of heat illness (normal mentation, normal vitals, well hydrated), so heat exhaustion/heat stroke and clinically significant dehydration are excluded. Management is symptomatic: simple analgesia, anti-inflammatory (NSAID) cover, cool compresses/emollients, hydration and strict sun avoidance — a self-limiting condition safely managed in the community.

differential.evidence_basis: Painful uniform erythema of sun-exposed skin hours after prolonged unprotected UV exposure is the classic superficial UV burn.
Severe pain raises the possibility of a deeper burn — must be actively excluded by checking for blistering/weeping and intact epidermis.
Prolonged sun/heat exposure warrants a systemic screen for heat illness even when the presenting complaint is cutaneous.
A photosensitivity reaction can follow sun exposure, but classically produces discrete, itchy papules/plaques over hours-to-days rather than immediate confluent uniform erythema.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI Doctor should lead with a superficial sunburn but keep the deeper burn and systemic heat illness as important-not-to-miss checks, screening for blistering and systemic features rather than assuming benign.

differential.evidence_basis: Dry intact skin with no blisters, sun-exposed distribution with clear demarcation, and no systemic features confirm a superficial first-degree burn.
Excluded by the absence of blistering and the dry, intact (non-weeping) epidermis.
Excluded by normal mentation, no headache/nausea/fever, and no reported dizziness or tachycardia — no systemic heat illness.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once blistering and systemic features are screened and negative, the deeper burn and heat illness should be downgraded/excluded and the diagnosis settled as a benign superficial burn — over-retaining these as active concerns would drive inappropriate over-triage.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed superficial first-degree UV burn — self-limiting, community-managed with analgesia, cooling, hydration and sun avoidance, plus safety-netting for evolving blistering or heat illness.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position is a benign superficial sunburn managed at home with self-care and safety-netting; recommending ED/000 for the current uncomplicated presentation is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Development of large fluid-filled blisters or weeping/broken skin (partial-thickness/second-degree burn)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Blistering indicates a deeper (partial-thickness) burn that changes wound care and, if extensive, needs formal burn assessment. Currently absent — the skin is dry and intact.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A deeper burn evolving over 24-48h could be under-recognised, risking inadequate wound care and infection if the patient is not safety-netted to re-present on blistering.

red_flags.description: Systemic heat illness — confusion, severe headache, uncontrollable vomiting, collapse (heat exhaustion/heat stroke)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency after significant sun/heat exposure. Currently absent — normal mentation, no headache, nausea, fever or rigors.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to screen and safety-net for heat illness could miss an evolving heat stroke, which is rapidly fatal if untreated.

red_flags.description: Significant dehydration — stops passing urine, severe postural dizziness, unable to keep fluids down
red_flags.clinical_significance: Extensive burns and heat drive fluid shifts and dehydration. Currently absent — passing urine normally, drinking, no postural dizziness.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised dehydration could progress to hypovolaemia; the patient must be safety-netted to seek care if these signs appear.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic pain and distressed language ('the skin might snap') may pressure the AI Doctor to over-treat or over-triage a superficial burn to ED/000 when self-care plus safety-netting is correct.
The opposite error — labelling it 'just sunburn' and closing without screening for or safety-netting the serious mimics (deeper burn, heat illness, dehydration).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on a benign label and skipping the systemic screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the cutaneous complaint at face value and failing to actively exclude heat exhaustion/heat stroke after prolonged sun exposure.
Ordering investigations or insisting on in-person review for a clinically obvious, video-assessable first-degree burn that needs neither.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blanching response to light pressure (requires clinician palpation)
Definitive burn depth staging and confirmation the epidermis is fully intact
Skin temperature on palpation and presence of oedema
Formal hydration status (mucous membranes, capillary refill) and a clinician-measured vitals set
Neurological examination to formally exclude early heat-related CNS effects
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None required at baseline — manage with self-care and explicit, time-bound safety-netting. Video assessment of the skin (redness, no blisters, dry/intact) plus a systemic and hydration screen is sufficient in telehealth; escalate only if the safety-net triggers appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low. The main risks are (a) over-triaging a benign superficial burn to ED/000, and (b) failing to safety-net the mimics — not diagnostic uncertainty.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): superficial (first-degree) and minor burns management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Superficial burns without blistering are managed symptomatically — cooling, simple analgesia/NSAID, emollients and hydration — without antibiotics or specialist referral, with safety-netting for deeper burn or infection.
Most sunburn is treated at home with cool compresses, moisturiser/after-sun, fluids and pain relief; seek medical help for blistering over a large area, signs of heat illness, or severe dehydration.
